title: Food Can Be Saved & Everything Connects
pillar_name: Regenerative Food Systems, Animal Stewardship & Land Care

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Bread Comes to Life: A Garden of Wheat and a Loaf to Eat
suggested.author: George Levenson

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Preserving Food Without Freezing or Canning
supplementary.author: the Gardeners and Farmers of Terre Vivante

competence_markers.description: The child has dried at least one type of food and stored it for later use
The child has helped freeze vegetables with proper blanching technique
The child has participated in making at least one fermented food (sauerkraut or similar)
The child understands that cool, dark storage extends the life of root vegetables and squash
The child can draw or describe at least three connections between different parts of the land system (garden-compost-animals-soil-water-food)

content.why_this_matters:
What happens to the food from the garden after you pick it? What about the eggs from the chickens? What about the apples from the tree when they all get ripe at the same time?

If you eat everything fresh, you eat well for a few weeks and then have nothing. If you learn to save food — to dry it, freeze it, ferment it, store it in a cool place — you can eat from your garden and your animals all year long, even in winter when nothing is growing.

People have been saving food for thousands of years. Long before refrigerators and freezers, families dried herbs and fruit in the sun, made pickles and sauerkraut by fermenting vegetables in salt, and stored root vegetables in cool underground cellars. These are not old-fashioned skills that nobody needs anymore. They are the skills that turn a summer garden into a year-round food supply.

This packet also asks you to start seeing something important: everything on the land is connected. The chickens eat bugs from the garden. Their manure goes in the compost. The compost feeds the garden. The garden grows food for your family and scraps for the chickens. It goes around and around in a circle, and when you start to see that circle, you are beginning to understand how a land system works.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Drying as preservation** — the simplest method, making dried herbs and fruit
2. **Freezing basics** — understanding that cold preserves food
3. **Fermentation introduction** — making sauerkraut or simple pickles and understanding that bacteria can be helpful
4. **Root cellaring concept** — storing food in cool, dark places to extend its life
5. **Systems awareness** — beginning to see how the garden, animals, compost, and kitchen connect
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Drying — The Oldest Way to Save Food

People have been drying food for thousands of years. In hot, dry climates, you can lay fruit on a flat rock in the sun and it dries by itself. In any climate, you can tie bundles of herbs and hang them upside down in a warm, dry room.

When you remove the water from food, bacteria and mold cannot grow. Dried food lasts for months — sometimes years — without spoiling.

**What you can dry:**
- **Herbs** — basil, oregano, thyme, rosemary, mint, dill. Tie small bundles with string and hang them upside down in a warm room with air circulation. In 1-2 weeks, they are dry and crumbly. Store in glass jars.
- **Fruit** — apple slices, pear slices, grapes (raisins!), peach slices, strawberries. Use a dehydrator or an oven set to its lowest temperature (120-140 degrees Fahrenheit). Dry until leathery — flexible but not sticky.
- **Vegetables** — tomatoes (sun-dried tomatoes), peppers, beans, corn. These take longer but store beautifully.

**Your activity:** Dry a batch of herbs from your garden. Tie the bundles, hang them, and check daily. When they are fully dry, crumble the leaves into jars and label them. Use them in cooking. You just preserved food using nothing but air.

### Part 2: Freezing — Using Cold to Stop Time

A freezer is a preservation tool. When food is frozen, the biological processes that cause it to spoil slow almost to a stop. A bag of frozen green beans from July can be eaten in January, and it will taste almost as fresh as the day it was picked.

**How to freeze vegetables:**
1. **Harvest at peak ripeness.** The quality of frozen food depends entirely on the quality of what you started with.
2. **Blanch.** Drop the vegetables into boiling water for 1-3 minutes (the time depends on the vegetable), then immediately transfer to ice water. Blanching stops the enzymes that cause flavor and color loss during freezing. It is a critical step — do not skip it.
3. **Dry and pack.** Spread blanched vegetables on a towel, pat dry, then pack into freezer bags. Remove as much air as possible.
4. **Label with the date.** You will not remember what it is or when it was frozen. Label everything.
5. **Freeze flat.** Lay bags flat in the freezer until frozen. Flat bags stack and organize better than lumpy ones.

**Your activity:** Help blanch and freeze a batch of vegetables from the garden — green beans, corn cut from the cob, or peas are good starters. Eat them in winter and notice that they taste like summer.

### Part 3: Fermentation — When Bacteria Are Your Friends

You have learned about bacteria in soil (2.5.1). Some bacteria help decompose organic matter. Some fix nitrogen. In fermentation, certain bacteria — called lactobacillus — do something remarkable: they eat the sugars in vegetables, produce lactic acid, and create an environment where harmful bacteria cannot survive. The result: food that is preserved, tangy, and actually more nutritious than the raw vegetable it started as.

**Making sauerkraut — your first fermentation:**

You need:
- One head of green cabbage
- 1 tablespoon of coarse salt (non-iodized — iodine kills the good bacteria)
- A clean Mason jar or fermentation crock
- Clean hands

Steps:
1. Remove the outer leaves of the cabbage. Cut in half, remove the core, and slice thin.
2. Put the sliced cabbage in a large bowl. Sprinkle salt over it.
3. Massage the cabbage with your hands. Squeeze, press, and work it. After 5-10 minutes, the cabbage will release water. This water is your brine.
4. Pack the cabbage tightly into the jar, pressing it down so the brine rises above the cabbage. The cabbage must be submerged — exposed cabbage will mold.
5. Cover loosely (gas needs to escape) and let it sit at room temperature.
6. After 1-2 days, you will see bubbles. That is the bacteria working — fermenting the sugars and producing lactic acid and carbon dioxide.
7. Taste it after 5-7 days. It should be tangy and crunchy. When it tastes good to you, put a lid on it and move it to the refrigerator.

You just made sauerkraut — one of the oldest preserved foods in the world, using nothing but cabbage, salt, and the bacteria that are already on the cabbage leaves.

### Part 4: Root Cellaring — Keeping Food Cool and Dark

Before refrigerators, every farmhouse had a root cellar — a cool, dark underground room where root vegetables, squash, apples, and fermented foods could be stored for months. The cool temperature (32-50 degrees Fahrenheit) and high humidity kept food fresh much longer than a kitchen shelf.

You may not have a root cellar. But you have cool places: an unheated basement, a garage (in cold months), a closet against an outside wall. Any place that stays cooler than your kitchen can extend the storage life of:
- **Potatoes** — cool, dark, moist. Do not store with apples (apples release ethylene gas that causes potatoes to sprout).
- **Carrots** — cool, moist. Layer in damp sand in a box.
- **Winter squash** — cool (50-55 degrees Fahrenheit), dry. Squash stores for months under the right conditions.
- **Apples** — cold (32-40 degrees Fahrenheit), moist. Wrap individually in newspaper to prevent one rotten apple from spoiling the rest.
- **Onions and garlic** — cool, dry, with good air circulation. Braid and hang, or store in mesh bags.

**Your activity:** Help store root vegetables from the garden harvest. Set up a storage area — even a cardboard box in a cool basement. Check on the stored food weekly. What is lasting? What is spoiling? Why?

### Part 5: Seeing the Connections

Now step back and look at everything you have learned in 2.5. How does it all connect?

- The **soil** feeds the plants (2.5.1)
- The **garden** grows food for your family (2.5.2)
- The **orchard** grows fruit for years (2.5.3)
- The **animals** produce eggs and meat, and their manure feeds the compost (2.5.4)
- The **compost** feeds the soil
- The **water** from rain feeds everything — and where it goes depends on how the land is managed (2.5.7)
- The **food** that is harvested can be preserved so you eat all year (2.5.8)

Draw this as a picture: the sun feeds the plants, the plants feed the people and animals, the animals feed the compost, the compost feeds the soil, the soil feeds the plants. Rain falls, water moves through the land, everything grows. And the food you preserve carries the summer's abundance into winter.

This circle — this connected system — is what a farm, a homestead, or even a backyard with a garden and a few chickens really is. Learning to see these connections is the beginning of thinking like a land manager.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Drying
**Developing well:** The child has dried at least one food item, checked on it during the drying process, and used the dried product in cooking or eating. They can explain that removing water preserves food.

**Developing slowly:** The child has not completed a drying project, or completed one but did not follow through to the using-the-dried-food step.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the activity been attempted? If not, it is the simplest preservation activity and should be done first. If attempted but abandoned, identify why and restart. Herb drying takes 1-2 weeks — the child needs to check periodically and see the transformation.

### 2. Freezing
**Developing well:** The child has helped blanch and freeze vegetables. They can explain that cold stops food from spoiling. They have eaten the frozen food later and noticed that it still tastes like the fresh version.

**Developing slowly:** The freezing activity was done entirely by an adult with the child watching, or the frozen food was never retrieved and eaten.

**Diagnostic questions:** Did the child participate in every step? Particularly blanching (with supervision for safety) and packing? And did the child eat the frozen food weeks or months later with awareness of where it came from?

### 3. Fermentation
**Developing well:** The child participated in making sauerkraut (or similar), observed the bubbling, tasted the result, and understands at a basic level that bacteria changed the cabbage. They enjoyed the process and the product.

**Developing slowly:** The child was not interested, or the fermentation failed (mold, off flavors), or the child refused to taste the result.

**Diagnostic questions:** If the fermentation failed, troubleshoot: was the cabbage fully submerged? Was the salt sufficient? Was the jar clean? A failed first attempt is common — try again. If the child will not taste it, do not force it — offer it with other foods and let curiosity develop. Many children are initially skeptical of fermented flavors and come around after repeated exposure.

### 4. Systems Awareness
**Developing well:** The child can describe at least three connections between different parts of the land system. They may spontaneously notice connections: "The chickens are eating the bugs from the garden!"

**Developing slowly:** The child sees each activity (gardening, animal care, composting, preserving) as separate and unrelated.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you been narrating the connections consistently? If not, start. The child learns systems thinking through hearing the connections articulated by someone who sees them. The "Connection Map" activity at the end of the packet is the test — but the narration throughout the packet is the teaching.
